Comparing Color Schemes: Color and Accessibility

Author
Affiliation

Yasmeen R’ghioui

George Washington University

Published

April 12, 2026

Table of contents

  • 1) Overview
    • 2) Environment setup
    • 3) GPA vs Offer Salary with Regression Line
      • 3a) GPA vs Offer Salary Random Sample
    • 4) Correlation Maxtrix of Numeric Variables
    • 5) Residual Plot Showcasing GPA and Offer Salary
  • 6) Reflection

1) Overview

The dataset contains 100,000 student records spanning demographics like major, university tier, and region, alongside academic indicators such as GPA and prior internship experience. It tracks the full application funnel from submissions to first and second round interviews, all the way to offers received. For students who secured an offer, additional details like salary, company size, and role relevance are included.

2) Environment setup

Code 
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import statsmodels.api as sm
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
import plotly.express as px
import altair as alt
data = pd.read_csv("job_data.csv")

3) GPA vs Offer Salary with Regression Line

Code 
data1 = data[["GPA", "Offer_Salary", "University_Rating"]].dropna()

sns.lmplot(
    data=data1,
    x="GPA",
    y="Offer_Salary",
    hue="University_Rating",
    height=5,
    aspect=1.2,
    ci=95,
    scatter_kws={"alpha": 0.1, "s": 20},
    line_kws={"linewidth": 4}
)

plt.title("GPA vs Offer Salary with Regression Line")
plt.show()

3a) GPA vs Offer Salary Random Sample

Code 
data_sample = data1.sample(500, random_state=42)

sns.lmplot(
    data=data_sample,
    x="GPA",
    y="Offer_Salary",
    hue="University_Rating",
    ci=95,
    height=5,
    aspect=1.2,
    scatter_kws={"alpha": 0.4, "s": 20},
    line_kws={"linewidth": 3}
)

4) Correlation Maxtrix of Numeric Variables

Code 
corr = data[["GPA", "Offer_Salary", "Applications_Submitted", 
             "Months_Searching", "Networking_Events_Attended", 
             "Prior_Internships"]].dropna().corr()

sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', center=0)
plt.title("Correlation matrix of numeric features")
plt.show()

5) Residual Plot Showcasing GPA and Offer Salary

Code 
sns.residplot(
    data=data.dropna(subset=["GPA", "Offer_Salary"]),
    x="GPA",
    y="Offer_Salary",
    lowess=True,
    color="#4e79a7",
    line_kws={"color": "red", "linewidth": 2}
)
plt.title("Residual Plot Showcasing GPA and Offer Salary")
plt.show()

6) Reflection

The scatter plot with regression lines showed that GPA has a good positive relationship with offer salary across all three university tiers, with top-tier students consistently earning higher predicted salaries than their mid- and lower-tier peers. However, the slopes across all three groups were surprisingly shallow, suggesting that GPA alone is not a strong driver of salary differences.

The correlation heatmap reinforced this finding. While GPA and offer salary showed a weak positive correlation of 0.13, the most notable relationship in the matrix was between applications submitted and months searching (0.64), indicating that students who search longer naturally submit more applications. Prior internships also showed a moderate positive correlation with offer salary, which was the strongest predictor of salary observed across the entire matrix.

The residual plot further questioned the strength of the linear model. The widespread residuals ranging from -40,000 to +60,000 and the non-flat lowess curve indicated substantial nonlinearity, confirming that a simple GPA-to-salary regression captures very little of the true variation in outcomes.

One challenge encountered was overplotting. The original lmplot using the full dataset was extremely crowded, making confidence intervals and individual trends hard to distinguish. Switching to a random sample of 500 observations using data1.sample(500, random_state=42) produced a much cleaner plot where the 95% confidence bands became clearly visible around each regression line, and the separation between university tiers was easier to interpret. This tradeoff highlighted how sample size affects visual clarity without necessarily changing the underlying story the data tells.
